Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Listen, I've lived eighty-one years and I've seen plenty of nonsense in my time, but this fake content business on social media is something else entirely. I check Facebook and Instagram maybe a few times a week to see what my children and their families are up to, and honestly, the amount of garbage people share without thinking is astounding. At the restaurant where I work, I hear customers discussing these wild stories they've read online, and half the time I want to tell them straight that they're being fooled, but that's not exactly good for tips. The thing about fake content is that people want to believe dramatic things. They see a shocking headline or some manipulated photo and immediately share it because it confirms what they already think. I learned long ago not to take everything at face value. When you've worked in service as long as I have, you develop a nose for when someone's feeding you a line. The same instinct applies online. If something seems designed to make you angry or scared, that's your first warning sign. I don't have fancy education beyond middle school, but common sense doesn't require a university degree. My approach is simple and direct. I don't share things unless I'm reasonably certain they're legitimate. If I see my children posting something questionable, I'll tell them plainly that they should check their sources. They don't always appreciate it, but I'm not here to be agreeable. I'm here to be honest. When I do come across obvious fake content, I usually just scroll past it. I'm not interested in getting into arguments with strangers online about whether some conspiracy theory is real. Life's too short, and I'd rather spend my limited time on social media looking at my grandchildren's photos or finding new painting techniques. The real problem is that social media platforms make money from engagement, whether that content is true or false. They're not particularly motivated to fix the problem. As an atheist, I don't have faith in divine intervention to sort this mess out, and I certainly don't have much faith in these tech companies doing the right thing either. What I do have faith in is individual responsibility. People need to slow down, think critically, and stop treating their share button like it's harmless. Every time you spread fake content, you're part of the problem. It's that straightforward.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an 81-year-old woman with lower secondary education, working part-time as a waitress in Milan. Her low agreeableness (10) suggests she would be direct, blunt, and not particularly concerned with being diplomatic. Her high openness (68) indicates curiosity and willingness to engage with new ideas like social media despite her age. Low neuroticism (9) means she's emotionally stable and not easily rattled. Moderate extraversion (64) suggests she's reasonably social but not overly so. Her limited time on social media (few times weekly) and practical work experience would inform a pragmatic, no-nonsense approach. Her atheism suggests reliance on rational thinking rather than faith-based reasoning. The tone should be direct, occasionally blunt, practical, and grounded in real-world experience rather than theoretical knowledge. She would draw on her life experience and work in service industry to illustrate points. Her moderate conscientiousness suggests she's reasonably careful but not obsessive about verification.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, blunt, pragmatic, no-nonsense, occasionally sharp, grounded in practical experience, unapologetic, straightforward
